failure_modes.text: Teams can treat delayed feedback as proof that an intervention failed and overcorrect before the system has time to respond.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams treat delayed feedback as proof that an intervention failed, then overcorrect before the system has had time to respond.
failure_modes.mitigation: Account for system response time before judging an intervention; overcorrecting before delayed feedback arrives creates unnecessary oscillation.

failure_modes.text: Waiting can become romanticized reflection when the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when people romanticize waiting and reflection even though the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an explicit wait threshold: if the next useful move is already clear, waiting is avoidance dressed as reflection.

failure_modes.text: Plans can quietly fail because people underestimate how often multi-step work will slip behind its intended timing.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Planning Fallacy (Failing to Anticipate Delay)
failure_modes.mitigation: Build delay buffers into multi-step plans; underestimating how often work slips behind its intended timing is the default human error.

premortem_questions.text: Are we reading no visible result yet as no effect, even though the system is known to respond with a lag?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when actions and consequences are separated in time, so naive short-term reading would mistake "no result yet" for "no effect".

premortem_questions.text: Is this pause improving judgment, or is it just avoiding commitment or ownership?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best used when the team must distinguish between a pause that improves judgment and a delay that merely avoids commitment or ownership.

premortem_questions.text: If one essential lag or capacity constraint is missing from our mental model, how would that distort the correction we are about to make?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Analyzing delayed feedback helps managers understand why their corrective actions might be too aggressive or too timid, leading to oscillation or drift in system performance.

heuristics.text: Map the delayed feedback path before changing course so you know when a response should realistically become visible.
heuristics.source_quote: Recognizing the location and length of time delays is key to successful system intervention.

heuristics.text: Chunk complex work into paced stages so delay becomes a learning aid instead of a source of overload.
heuristics.source_quote: Since working memory is a limited resource, complex topics must be broken down temporally, focusing on steps or stages in a sequence.

heuristics.text: Set aggressive but achievable deadlines when the true problem is self-generated sprawl rather than useful incubation time.
heuristics.source_quote: Setting aggressive (but achievable) deadlines forces prioritization and helps free the mind from the sustained background stress of a distant deadline.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on lagged feedback, paced learning, and anti-overreaction discipline rather than on broad productivity advice.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor scheduling sprawl because the raw source is stronger on delayed feedback, cognitive pacing, and overcorrection risk.
open_questions: The third premortem question is normalized from the systems-delay framing so it stays reusable across operational and learning contexts.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on systems-thinking, leverage-points, and cognitive-load-theory for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strongest on urgency and present-bias pressure, but the structured tension block names the cleanest current-vocabulary conflicts.
structured_tensions: The source contains an explicit structured tension section that should survive into Wave 3 with bounded normalization.
